1.synthesis: Crossing to the Gentile shore, Jesus is met by a man no one could bind, who lived among the tombs, crying out and cutting himself (vv.1–5). The unclean spirits, naming themselves "Legion, for we are many" (v.9), beg to enter a herd of pigs, which rush down the bank and drown (vv.11–13). The townspeople find the man "clothed and in his right mind" and, afraid, beg Jesus to leave (vv.15–17). The healed man wants to follow, but Jesus sends him instead to proclaim "how much the Lord has done for you" — and he publishes it through the Decapolis (vv.18–20). The second of the four great miracles shows Jesus' authority reaching across the boundary into Gentile darkness.
1.historical_context: The Decapolis, east of the lake, was largely Gentile territory — the herd of pigs (v.11) confirms it, since pig-keeping was unthinkable in Jewish land. The man's home among the tombs was a legal as well as social exile: contact with the dead made one ritually unclean (Numbers 19:16), so he lived in perpetual impurity, cut off from every institution. "Legion" (v.9) is pointedly Roman — a legion numbered some six thousand soldiers — so the demonic power bears the name of the occupying army, an image Mark's Roman readers would feel. The drowning herd (v.13) faintly echoes the destruction of Pharaoh's army in the sea (Exodus 14:28), and the great economic loss explains the town's fearful request that Jesus leave (v.17).
1.literary_structure: This is the most elaborate exorcism in Mark, structured as a power-encounter: the man no chain could bind (vv.3–4) meets the One who binds the strong man (Mark 3:27), and Legion, which none could subdue, is itself cast out. The crossing to "the other side" (v.1) immediately follows the storm-stilling (Mark 4:35–41), pairing authority over chaos at sea with authority over the chaos of the demonic. Strikingly, Jesus here reverses his usual command to silence: where in Jewish territory he muzzles testimony (Mark 5:43), in the Gentile Decapolis he commissions proclamation (v.19), since the messianic secret guards against premature royal expectation among Jews, not Gentiles.
1.theological_application: The gospel moves toward the tombs — toward the most catastrophically isolated and unclean — for Christ's first act in Gentile territory is to seek and save the man everyone else had chained and abandoned (v.2; cf. Luke 19:10). The town's reaction is a sobering warning: faced with the cost of one man's liberation, they prefer their pigs to his Deliverer and ask Jesus to leave (vv.16–17). The restored man models the proper response — he wants to be "with him," and when sent instead, he obeys and tells (vv.18–20), becoming the first missionary in Mark.
1.christology: The demons confess what the disciples have not yet seen: "Jesus, Son of the Most High God" (v.7) — using the Gentile name for God (cf. Genesis 14:18) and recognizing the One with authority to torment the tormentors. Their forced self-naming (v.9) shows Christ's mastery over organized hosts of evil, not merely single spirits. And his commission — "tell them how much the Lord has done for you" (v.19) — which the man fulfills by proclaiming "how much Jesus had done" (v.20), quietly equates Jesus with the Lord himself. The crossing to the Gentile shore is the incarnation in miniature: holiness entering the place of uncleanness to set captives free.

21.pericope_label: Jairus's Daughter and a Sick Woman
21.synthesis: Back on the Jewish shore, Jairus, a synagogue ruler, falls at Jesus' feet pleading for his dying daughter, and Jesus goes with him (vv.22–24). On the way a woman with a twelve-year hemorrhage, having spent all she had on physicians, touches his garment in the crush, thinking, "If I touch even his garments, I will be made well" — and immediately she is healed (vv.25–29). Jesus, aware that power has gone out from him, will not let her remain anonymous; he draws out her trembling confession and sends her away whole: "Daughter, your faith has made you well; go in peace" (vv.30–34). The interruption itself becomes a story of faith reaching through shame to Christ.
21.historical_context: Jairus's standing as a synagogue ruler (v.22) makes his public prostration before Jesus remarkable, given the leadership's growing hostility. The woman's condition was socially devastating: a chronic flow of blood rendered her ritually unclean and everything she touched impure (Leviticus 15:25–27), so twelve years meant twelve years of untouchable isolation from worship and community. To press into a crowd and touch a teacher's clothing was a serious violation and a real risk (vv.27–28). Her hope in the garment reflects a widespread belief that healing power could pass through a holy person's clothes; Jewish tradition associated such power with the tassels of the garment (cf. "healing in its wings," Malachi 4:2).
21.literary_structure: This is a classic Markan intercalation: the Jairus story is split open and the woman's healing inserted, so the two interpret each other. The links are deliberate — "twelve years" of illness (v.25) against the twelve-year-old girl (Mark 5:42), two females, two encounters at the boundary of death and life, and Jesus addressing the woman as "Daughter" (v.34) just before he reaches Jairus's daughter. The delay caused by the woman's healing is not incidental: it raises the stakes for Jairus, turning a healing into a resurrection. The address "Daughter" — an adult term of belonging — publicly welcomes the unclean outcast into the family of those who belong to Jesus.
21.theological_application: The woman's faith is neither magical nor superstitious but a desperate, specific trust in a specific person (v.28), and Jesus honors it — yet he refuses to leave her healing impersonal. He calls her out, not to shame her, but to turn a stolen touch into a face-to-face relationship and a public restoration: "go in peace" (v.34) declares her wholeness and renewed standing before the very crowd that had excluded her. The scene encourages all who feel too unclean or unnoticed to come: Christ both heals and names, drawing trembling faith into the open.
21.christology: Power flows from Jesus at the mere touch of his garment (v.30), revealing him as the source of a healing virtue that the law's contagion of impurity cannot defile — instead of her uncleanness defiling him, his wholeness cleanses her. He perceives the one touch amid the pressing throng (vv.30–32), a quiet display of more-than-human knowledge. And in calling a ritually unclean, impoverished, anonymous woman "Daughter" before a synagogue ruler (v.34), he enacts the heart of his mission: to gather the excluded into the household of God.

35.synthesis: While Jesus still speaks to the woman, word comes: "Your daughter is dead. Why trouble the Teacher any further?" (v.35). Jesus answers Jairus with the call that governs the whole scene: "Do not fear, only believe" (v.36). Taking only Peter, James, and John, he enters the house of mourning, dismisses the wailing crowd who laugh at his words "the child is not dead but sleeping," and takes her by the hand: "Talitha cumi" — "Little girl, I say to you, arise" (vv.37–41). She rises and walks, and amid their amazement he orders silence and tells them to give her something to eat (vv.42–43). The fourth great miracle brings Jesus' authority to its furthest reach — over death itself.
35.historical_context: The messengers' practical grief — death is final, so why burden the healer with a corpse (v.35) — frames the impossibility Jesus is about to overturn. The professional mourners already wailing at the house (v.38) reflect customary Jewish funeral practice, begun immediately upon death. "Talitha cumi" (v.41) preserves the actual Aramaic words of Jesus in transliteration — one of several such retentions in Mark (cf. Mark 7:34; Mark 14:36) — consistent with the early tradition that Mark preserved Peter's eyewitness memory, the very syllables spoken. The instruction to feed her (v.43) confirms she is bodily, hungrily alive, not a ghost.
35.literary_structure: This completes the intercalation begun at v.21, and Jesus' word to Jairus — "Do not fear, only believe" (v.36) — deliberately echoes his rebuke in the storm, "Have you still no faith?" (Mark 4:40), placing Jairus in the disciples' position: trust when circumstances forbid it. The selection of the inner three (v.37) marks this as one of the privileged disclosures granted only to Peter, James, and John (cf. the Transfiguration, Gethsemane). The euphemism "sleeping" (v.39) and the strict command to secrecy (v.43) extend the messianic secret to its sharpest point — even a raising must, for now, be hushed.
35.theological_application: To Jairus, and to every believer at the edge of the unbearable, Jesus says, "Do not fear, only believe" (v.36) — faith is not denial of the facts but trust in the One who is greater than them. That Jesus calls death "sleep" (v.39) reframes the grave for those who are his: what is final to the mourners is reversible to him. The tender particulars — taking her hand, ordering food (vv.41–43) — show that his power serves love, anchoring the miraculous in homely care for a child.
35.christology: Jesus speaks to the dead as easily as to the living, and his bare word raises her (v.41) — the prerogative of the Author of life, who treats death as a sleep from which he can wake whom he will. The progression of Mark's four signs reaches its summit here: authority over the storm, the demons, incurable disease, and now death itself, building the unanswered question "Who then is this?" (Mark 4:41) toward its only possible answer. This raising is a foretaste and pledge of his own resurrection and of the final "arise" he will speak over all who sleep in him.
